ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of synthesis process of CQDs from Aegle marmelos leaves.

(a) TEM image of the synthesized CQD sample, the TEM image presented in the inset is taken at a higher magnification, depicts the existence of spherical CQDs. (b) HRTEM image of individual CQDs is marked with yellow circles, the HRTEM image in high magnification (in inset) depicts the crystalline interior of a single CQD. (c) Raman spectra of the synthesized sample at an excitation wavelength of 514.6nm. (d) XPS survey spectrum of synthesized CQDs and (e) The high resolution XPS spectrum corresponding to C1s peak. (f) FTIR spectrum of the sample.

(a) UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption (i) and PL emission spectra collected at an excitation wavelength of 325nm of the synthesized CQDs (1mgmL−1) in an aqueous medium (ii). (b) Excitation-dependent PL emission spectra collected at excitation wavelengths ranging from 275nm to 400nm measured at room temperature. (c) Normalized PL emission spectra of synthesized CQDs dispersed in CH (i), EtOH (ii) and DI water (iii) solvents are collected at an excitation wavelength of 325nm. (d) Schematic for PL emission process in nonpolar and polar solvents. (e) Variation of PL quantum yield of the synthesized CQDs and permittivity of the polar dispersant medium, the inset shows the corresponding digital camera images of the sample dispersed in (i) DI water, (ii) EtOH and (iii) THF under 365nm UV irradiation.

Schematic of the indigenously developed experimental set up for the measurement of the emission spectrum of PVA-CQD film immersed in different organic liquids for 1min and under irradiation of 365nm UV radiation. The measured emission spectra and the digital photograph of the PVA-CQD film in different media have been shown in the figure marked as (a) and (b), respectively.

(a) PL emission response of the CQDs aqueous solution (0.1mgmL−1) loaded with different metal ions with a concentration of 1mM (b) PL emission spectra of CQDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions ranging from 0μM to 300μM. (c) Variation of PL quenching efficiency (I0/I) with concentration of Fe3+ within a range of 0–300μM. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CQDs in presence of different concentration of Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 250μM. (e) Two separate region of the data point shown in (c) is plotted in figures (i) for 0–20μM and (ii) 25–300μM.
Mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CQDs in presence of Fe3+ ions.

Solvation parameters of the solvents used in this study in their increasing order of dielectric constant (ε) [62], α is the hydrogen bond donating (HBD) capacity of the solvent commonly known as Kamlet-Taft polarity parameter [64], ET N is the molecular-microscopic solvent polarity parameter and ET (kcal mol−1) is the molar electronic transition energy of the solvents [62].
A comparison of the performance of as-synthesized CQDs with some of the Carbon dot based luminescent probes for detection of Fe3+ ions reported earlier.
Recoveries of Fe3+ ions in tap water samples detected by proposed method (n=3).

head.ce:title: Solvatochromism in highly luminescent environmental friendly carbon quantum dots for sensing applications: Conversion of bio-waste into bio-asset

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscience Laboratory, Dept. of Physics, National Institute of Technology Durgapur, 713209, West Bengal, India

ce:section.ce:para: Since the first report of discovery of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles in 2004, the search for benign carbon based nanomaterials having optical properties similar to those of semiconductor quantum dots are still continuing [1]. And recently significant research interests have been seen on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) due to their fascinating optical properties which come from π-conjugated (sp2 hybridized) graphitic backbone in the presence of sp3 hybridized functional groups [2–10]. Luminescent CQDs, in compared to the conventional luminescent organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, exhibit some advantageous properties, such as easy and low-cost synthesis procedures, availability of easy methods of surface modification, high photostability, high aqueous solubility and inherent biocompatibility [2–3,7]. And these exceptional characteristics of CQDs may be utilized advantageously in designing optode for biophotonic and bio-sensing applications [4–5]. So far, a variety of techniques such as, arc discharge [1], microwave irradiation [6], electrochemical oxidation [8] and oxygen plasma treatment [9] have been developed for synthesis of such highly luminescent CQDs.

However, the previous synthesis procedures as mentioned above are not the ideal choice for greener production of luminescent CQDs, as most of these techniques rely either on the use of strong chemical reagents [5] or require complicated reaction constraints/ mechanisms or both. In most of the earlier mentioned methods [1–4,6–9] additional expensive surface passivation agents are required in order to enhance the fluorescent quantum yield of the synthesized luminescent nano carbons to achieve the goals of the application. Recently, Liu et al. [5] have reported sensing application of carbon dots synthesized by chromatographic fractions technique. Whereas, Ali et al., have reported synthesis of hydrophobic fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) from ascorbic acid and oleylamine solution [10] and then CNPs are turned into hydrophilic one by functionalization with PEG, primary amine, glucose, etc. However, a drastic difference in PL quantum yield (PLQY) between hydrophilic and hydrophobic CNPs is reported [10]. However, green and facile approaches for the synthesis of highly luminescent biocompatible CQDs with interesting optical properties along-with their fluorescent based sensing applications is rarely reported. Therefore, hydrothermal method for synthesis of CQDs has gained much-anticipated attention among the scientific community due to its cheap and green approach compared to other synthesis techniques [2–3]. In addition to that, luminescent carbon nanostructures can also be synthesized successfully from different bio-precursors by using the hydrothermal technique and one can avoid the requirements of complicated post-synthesis treatments.

Recently, there are some reports on green syntheses of luminescent carbon nanostructures for different biophotonic sensing applications [11,12]. The natural occurrence of fluorescent CQDs in honey has been reported very recently by Mandani et al. [13]. And such study has propelled researchers to design and manufacture of more sustainable and eco-friendly soft carbon nanostructures for more reliable applications in bio-photonics. However, further study on designing and synthesis of functional nanomaterials for highly reliable sensing of organic compounds and toxic ionic environment has a major space in terms of technological aspects. The exhaustive production and study on luminescent sensing probes with innovative detection parameters are more desirable [14–17] than the conventional analytical techniques, such as Karl-Fisher titration [18], gas chromatography [19] in terms of selectivity, low background noise, easy fabrication procedure and cost-effective instrumental requirements [20–22]. However, due to the possibility of tailored designing of fluorescent nanostructures, highly sensitive and selective detection of analytes by using nano fluorescent probe has become a most effective technique now-a-days. Most importantly, luminescent carbon nanostructures in photo-excited state, is an reliable candidate for the purpose of fluorescence based sensing applications as it can act as a good electron donor or accepter [3], especially in the case where electron acceptor or donating ions, solvents and gases are the detection targets.

The time resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) experiment have been utilized earlier by some researchers to find the effects of energy redistribution, mechanisms of relaxation among the emitting states, and the origin of spectral migration of the fluorescence spectra of CQDs [23–26]. TRPL measurement results can also be utilized while using CQDs in sensing applications. Recently, Kalytchuk et al. have reported monotonic shortening of the apparent PL lifetime of carbon dots with increasing temperature and they have utilized their temperature-dependent PL lifetime data to demonstrate the application of carbon dots in intracellular nano-thermometry [26]. Another interesting phenomenon, the ability of fluorophore to change their absorption and/or emission spectra as a result of a change in solvent polarity, known as “solvatochromism”, can be utilized industriously for the purpose of sensing applications [27,28]. More importantly, the interaction between the polar functional groups attached to the surface of a fluorophore having a π-conjugated backbone as well as those present in the dispersed medium may play key roles in achieving the solvatochromic effect. The impact of π-conjugated structures grafted with electron donor and acceptor functional groups on solvent polarity dependent fluorescence property has been reported earlier [29–31]. Recently, solvatochromic effect of a new π-conjugated dye based on phenylene(poly)ethylene for sensitive detection of low level of water in organic solvents has been demonstrated by Guan et al. [31]. Although, during the last a few decades, solvatochromic behaviors of some typical dyes and their derivatives have been studied enormously, only a few of them have been applied in practice due to a number of limitations in terms of sensitivity, photostability, chemical stability and toxicity [31–33].

Observation of solvatochromic effect in dispersible and highly luminescent semiconductor quantum dots of CdSe has been reported by Leatherdale et al. [34] . However, in-vivo application of CdSe as a fluorescent sensing probe is limited due to their toxic nature and further modification in structural and chemical parameters are essentially needed [35]. Solvatochromism in single-walled carbon nanotubes has also been reported by Choi et al. [36] . However, carbon nanotubes still have several drawbacks, such as their insolubility in most organic solvents [37] and required functionalization for achieving the hydrophilicity which limits their hands-on application as a sensing probe [38]. Thus new approach for synthesis of highly luminescent, water dispersible and bio-compatible novel materials having solvatochromic PL emission property for fluorescence based polarity and ionic sensing applications is urgently needed. Solvatochromic PL (SPL) emission in fluorescent carbon nanostructures including in CQDs is a rarely reported phenomenon and only a handful of reports are available, so far [29,39–41]. However, recently, Sciortino et al. [40] have used solvatochromism as an important tool to find the origin of excitation dependent PL emissive behavior of heavily N-doped CQDs with carbon nitride core. Therefore, a fresh investigation on SPL and optical properties of CQDs, synthesized by using bio-based precursor material will be very interesting, in view of its fundamental aspects as well as for demonstrating fluorescence based sensing applications.

In the present study, we have used an eco-friendly approach i.e., hydrothermal technique for synthesis of water soluble, self-passivated luminescent CQDs from naturally available bio-waste, namely Aegle marmelos (popularly known in India and in some other countries as “Bael”) leaves powder and a detailed study on SPL emission property of CQDs has been carried out for sensing applications. The luminescent CQDs having 3–8nm sizes have exhibited excitation dependent PL emission behavior with PLQY of 3% in an aqueous medium. Interestingly, an enhancement in PLQY up to 22% has been achieved by dispersing the CQDs in tetrahydrofuran (THF) with permittivity (ε)=7.5. Also the PLQY of the synthesized CQDs has been found to follow an exponential decaying nature with increase in ε of different polar dispersants. It has been found that the excitation wavelength (λex ) dependent PL emission property of the CQDs in an aqueous medium is strongly related to the solvent polarity, when λex is >325nm. Whereas, PL emission behavior of the sample for λex < 325nm has been found to be dominated by the concentration dependent rigidity aggregated crosslinking enhanced emission (RACEE). The observed RACEE is occurring due to the presence of functional groups (act as sub-fluorophore) within the π-conjugated graphitic core of CQDs. To find the applicability of the CQDs as a highly precise fluorescent sensing probe, the photo-physical stability of the synthesized sample has also been tested by varying different experimental conditions.

A flexible PVA-CQD film has also been developed to demonstrate the solvent sensitive emission characteristics of the synthesized sample in solid state form, under irradiation of 365nm UV light. By using an indigenously developed experimental setup it has been shown that the PVA-CQD solid and flexible luminescence system emits intensity modulated blue light (~450nm) when it is immersed in bulk organic solvents of diverse polarities. This observation supports the fact that PVA-CQD flexible film can be used as a solid state photonic sensor for detection of organic liquids in future. On the other hand, the SPL emission property of the synthesized bare CQDs sample, at an excitation wavelength of 325nm have been demonstrated after dispersing it in different non-polar (such as cyclohexene, CH), polar protic (such as, MeOH, EtOH and DI water) and polar aprotic solvents (such as THF, dimethylsulfoxide, acetone and DMF,). The SPL emissions of CQDs in polar aprotic solvents have been found to occur due to non-specific dipole-dipole interaction in accordance with the Lippert-Mataga model. Whereas, when CQDs are dispersed in polar protic solvent, hydrogen bonding (HB) along-with dipole-dipole interactions have been found to play important roles.

Here, we have demonstrated the detection of organic solvents namely, EtOH and THF from their aqueous binary mixture by using the SPL emission behavior of the synthesized material and achieved the sensitivity of 1% and 0.9% molar fraction for EtOH and THF, respectively. Furthermore, the effects of the presence of different metal ions (Zn2+, Ag+, Al3+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+) on PL emission characteristics of the aqueous dispersion of CQDs have been studied in the present work. PL emission intensity of the CQDs loaded with Fe3+ ions has been found to be quenched to the maximum value of 30% as compared to that of the unloaded CQDs. Whereas the PL emission intensity of the CQD loaded with other metal ions remains almost unaltered. This observation suggests the selectivity of the synthesized carbon nanostructures towards Fe3+ ions. We have also demonstrated possible application of CQDs in Fe3+ ion sensing even in real water samples with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.12μM. Thus in the present study we have demonstrated the conversion of a bio-waste to a bio-asset via green synthesis approach and synthesized highly luminescent CQDs. The detailed investigation on SPL emission property of the samples has added a new dimension to the low-cost synthesis of CQDs and its fluorescent based sensing applications and it could be an ideal replacement of traditional dye molecules and semiconductor nanostructures in future. On the other hand, demonstration of solvent sensitive emissive behavior of flexible PVA-CQD films can provide a new concept of polarity sensing by using CQDs in solid state form.

The fresh “Bael” green leaves used as precursor for the synthesis of CQDs has been collected from National Institute of Technology (NIT) Durgapur campus (India). Silver nitrate (AgNo3), Iron chloride (FeCl3
. 6H2O), Cadmium nitrate (Cd(NO3)2
. 4H2O), Aluminium nitrate (Al(NO3)3
. 9H2O), Zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2
. 6H2O), Manganese chloride (MnCl2
. 2H2O), Copper sulphate (CuSO4
. 5H2O), Nickel Chloride (NiCl2
. 6H2O), Methanol (MeOH), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), Tetrahydrofuran (THF), Acetone (Ace) and Cyclohexane (CH) have been purchased from Merck. Ethyl alcohol (EtOH) has been purchased from TEDIA. Deionized water (DI water) has been used in the experiments while preparation of aqueous dispersions of the sample for abosprtion and PL measuerments.

Fluorescent CQDs have been synthesized by a hydrothermal technique by using bael leaves as a source of carbon. Initially, raw and fresh leaves have been washed with tap water and then with DI Water. Then the cleaned leaves are chopped into small pieces and kept for drying for two days at 50°C. The dried bael leaves are then crushed into fine powder by using mortar. To make an extract of precursor material, 2g of bael leaf powder has been dissolved in 60mL of 0.2% aqueous solution of NaOH (0.05molmL−1) and then heated at 80°C for 30min. The obtained dark yellowish liquid is then kept at room temperature for cooling followed by filtration to discard the suspended bulk counterparts. After that an amount of 30mL of the filtered yellowish liquid is transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave of 100mL capacity and heated at 170°C for 5h. Thereafter, the obtained colloidal dark brownish suspension has been collected and washed several times with ethanol to remove the unreacted organic components, if any. The resulting solution has been centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 15min to separate the solvent and finally dried at 80°C to get the CQDs in solid form. The CQDs thus obtained have been re-dispersed in DI water for different optical and structural characterizations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Solid Flexible PVA-CQD Film
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prepare PVA-CQD film we have followed the same procedure as described elsewhere [11]. To prepare the PVA-CQD solid film 2mL of aqueous CQD (4mgmL−1) solution has been mixed with 5mL PVA solution (10wt%) and the resulting mixture is then stirred vigorously for 1h to form the homogenous PVA-CQD hydrogel. The PVA-CQD hydrogel is then kept in a petri dish at ~50°C for 12h. The solid, transparent and flexible PVA-CQD films have been peeled off carefully from the petri dish and used for further application purpose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Detection of Organic Solvents in the Aqueous Binary Mixture
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of water-miscible organic solvents such as, ethanol and THF have been carried out by using the SPL property of the synthesized CQDs. Firstly, we have prepared aliquots (5mL) of aqueous binary mixtures containing different molar fraction of ethanol ranging from 0 to 0.05. The synthesized CQDs are then loaded (0.1mgmL−1) with each of the aqueous binary mixture aliquots followed by ultrasonication for 15min. The PL emission spectra of as prepared water-ethanol mixtures loaded with CQDs have been recorded at room temperature at an excitation wavelength of 325nm (photon energy 3.28eV). A similar procedure has been followed for the aqueous binary mixture of THF to demonstrate the solvatochromic detection of THF in water by using the synthesized CQDs.

The fluorescence detection of Fe3+ has been carried out by using synthesized CQDs dispersed in DI water at room temperature. Briefly, an appropriate amount of Fe3+ of different concentration ranging from 0 to 300μM has been added to a 3mL aqueous solution of CQDs (0.1mgmL−1), stirred and incubated for 30min. The PL emission spectrum of each of the CQD samples loaded with different concentration of Fe3+ are then measured at an excitation wavelength of 325nm. Moreover, to examine the selectivity of the synthesized CQDs for detection of Fe+3, CQDs at a concentration of 0.1mgmL−1 has been added with 1mM aqueous solution of different metal cations (Fe+3, Ag+, Cd+2, Al+3, Zn+2, Mn+2, Cu+2 and Ni+2) and their effect on the PL emission characteristics has been studied by measuring PL emission spectra.

Furthermore, to demonstrate sensing performance of the luminescent CQD as a sensing probe for detection Fe3+ in the environmental sample, tap water samples have been collected from two different places i.e., from Nanoscience laboratory (Sample 1) and from Department of Physics, NIT Durgapur (Sample 2). The collected water samples are then used as a replacement of DI water during the metal ion detection experiment by using CQDs. The tap water samples have been spiked with different concentration of Fe3+ (50μM, 100μM and 200μM) and added with sensing probe (CQDs, 0.1mgmL−1) followed by measurement of resultant PL emission spectra at an excitation wavelength of 325nm.

The morphological and structural characterization of the synthesized CQD samples have been analyzed by using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEOL JEM 2100) operated at accelerating voltage of 200kV. And prior to HRTEM analyses, the samples have been dropped on a copper grid coated with amorphous carbon and dried under ambient condition. The microstructural characterization of the synthesized materials have been done with Raman spectroscopic measurement (Lab RAM HR Jovin Yvon) by using argon‑krypton mixed ion gas laser of wavelength 514.6nm as excitation. Moreover, for optical characterization, the UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of the as-synthesized CQDs have been recorded by using a double beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (HITACHI U-3010). The PL emission characteristics of the sample have been measured at room temperature by using a spectrofluorimeter (Perkin Elmer LS55) equipped with xenon flash lamp acts as a source of excitation. In order to analyze the functional groups present on the surface of the synthesized sample, Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectroscopic measurement has been carried out in powder form by using an IR-spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet 6700). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the synthesized sample has been performed by using the PHI 5000 Verse Probe II system. The micro-focussed (100μm, 25W, 15 KV) monochromatic Al-Kα source (hν=1486.6eV) with a hemispherical analyzer and a multichannel detector has been used during the XPS measurement.

Aegle marmelos (bael) is a natural herb and its leaves are commonly used around the world as a natural medicinal product. In the present study, Water soluble, self-passivated luminescent and solvatochromic CQDs have been synthesized by using simple hydrothermal process by using Aegle marmelos leaves as a starting material. The typical synthesis procedure has been schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . The initial change in colour of precursor solution from dark yellow to dark brown after hydrothermal synthesis process confirmed the successful preparation of luminescent CQDs via hydrothermal carbonization [42].

TEM micrographs of the samples are shown in Fig. 2a–b. Fig. 2a depicts a typical TEM image of poly-dispersed CQD and its inset shows TEM image of the sample taken at a higher magnification, which reveals the formation of spherical CQDs (marked with white circular rings) having size distribution of ~3–8nm. Moreover, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images have been taken and those are displayed in Fig. 2b. HRTEM image of individual CQDs as represented by the yellow circles in Fig. 2b has been shown and it shows clearly that the synthesized materials are consisting with a sp2 hybridized highly crystalline core. Furthermore, the lattice parameter of ~0.22nm, corresponding to the interlayer spacing of (100) facet of graphitic lattice [43] has been estimated from the HRTEM observation, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2b.

Raman spectrum of the sample has also been collected at an excitation wavelength of 514.6nm laser radiation and it is shown in Fig. 2c along-with the fitted curves. It is found that the Raman spectrum consist of two peaks, one at ~1381cm−1, corresponding to D band and other at ~1510cm−1, corresponding to the G band of the disordered graphitic structures, [44,45] which has been further fitted by the Gaussian (fitted peak 1) and Lorentzian (fitted peak 2) line shape functions, respectively. Under visible light excitation these G and D bands have been excited due to the combination of vibrational modes induced due to the effect of ordered sp2 hybridized graphitic core and disordered sp3 hybridized functional groups attached on the edge of the graphitic core, respectively [42]. In order to quantify the presence of disordered states, the intensity ratio of D band and G band (i.e., ID/IG ) has been calculated. Unlike the previous cases, [11,46] in the present study, the value of ID/IG is found out to be 0.6, which is smaller than 1. However, the value of ID/IG in the present study is much larger compared to that of the pure graphitic structures having ID/IG of ~0.365 [47]. This result suggests the formation of CQDs having highly crystalline sp2 hybridized π-conjugated graphitic core along-with the disordered surface states.

XPS and FTIR spectroscopic measurement results are presented in Fig. 1d–f. The XPS survey spectrum is shown in Fig. 2d, which depicts the presence of three peaks at ~284, 399 and 530eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The peaks at ~60 and 496eV have been appeared due to the presence of Mg and Ca, respectively [47]. The appearances of these elements within the synthesized CQD sample have occurred due to the presence of trace elements within the leaf extract used as precursor during the synthesis of CQDs [47]. The C1s core spectrum has been further deconvoluted into three contributions (Fig. 2e), corresponding to conjugated sp2 hybridized carbon (C=C) at a binding energy of 284eV (85%), sp3 hybridized carbon (CO, CN) at 286eV (10%) and CN, CO bonds at 288eV (5%) [7]. The high resolution XPS spectrum corresponding to O1s and N1s peaks have been shown in Fig. S1a–b (see Supporting information). As depicted in Fig. S1a, the O1s spectrum has been deconvoluted into two peaks at 534eV and 530eV, which are due to the presence of OCOH and CO bonds, respectively [5,7]. The N1s spectrum, as shown in Fig. S1b, is consisting with two main band at 399eV and 401eV, which are assigned to pyridinic N (CNC) and amidic N bonds, respectively [5]. The presences of these oxygen and nitrogen containing surface functional groups have been confirmed further by FTIR data analyses. In the FTIR spectrum, shown in Fig. 2f, the existence of absorption bands due to several functional groups, such as OH, NH, CC and CO are clearly observed. Notably, the broad IR absorption band at 3636–3209cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of OH and NH groups [48]. The week absorption band centered at ~2932cm−1 is due to the CH stretching mode of vibration. Whereas, the sharp absorption band appeared in 1630–1522cm−1 has been attributed to the stretching vibrational modes contributed by CO and aromatic CC bonds [48,49] and the bending modes of vibrations due to the presence of NH groups present within the synthesized sample. Thankfully, the existence of aromatic CC confirms the carbonization of the sample occurred during the hydrothermal treatment. In addition, the IR absorption band centered at ca. 1076cm−1 has been appeared due to stretching mode of vibration of CO bonds [48]. The occurrence of these functional groups is due to the presence of different bioactive alkaloids (Skimmianine, Agelin) within the precursor used during the synthesis process and it renders the hydrophilic nature of the CQDs [49] and promotes their interactions with different solvents as well as with solid polymer matrix. In earlier reports, the electronic states which are occurred within the HUMO-LUMO gap due the presence of surface functional groups have been found to be a decisive factor for achieving excitation dependent PL emission in fluorescent carbon nanostructures [3,36]. In the present study, the charge transfer dynamics between the photo excited energy states of CQDs and surrounding solvents are found to be important for observing the linear optical behavior of CQDs including its SPL emission property. And therefore a convenient “mix and detect” [3] protocol for detection of organic solvents (EtOH and THF) and Fe3+ ions have been demonstrated by using CQDs as a fluorescent nano-probe.

The linear optical absorption and PL emission characteristics of the synthesized CQDs have been depicted in Fig. 3a–d. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the synthesized CQDs dispersed in DI water (Fig. 3a(i)) clearly depicts the presence of one sharp absorption band centered at ~270nm and another shoulder absorption band at ~314nm along-with the absorption tail till 550nm. The presence of sharp absorption band centered at ~270nm has been attributed to the π-π* transition of electrons in π-conjugated sp2 carbon core. Whereas, the shoulder absorption band at ~314nm has been associated to the n-π* electronic transitions in non-bonding orbitals due to the functional groups attached to sp2 hybridized carbon core [4,42]. The absorption tail till 550nm has been assigned to an ensemble of low energy transitions within the surface states which are created due to the presence of functional groups attached to the edge of sp2 hybridized graphitic carbon core [50].

The synthesized material exhibits excitation dependent PL emission characteristics. As shown in Fig. 3b, the PL peak emission intensity centered at ~432nm is increased with the excitation wavelength and becomes the highest at an excitation wavelength of 325nm and then it is decreased with further increase in excitation wavelength. Not only the intensity of the PL peak emission but also a large shift in the center of the PL peak emission (λem ) from 432nm towards longer wavelength has been observed when the excitation wavelength has been varied from 325nm to 400nm (Fig. 3b). This excitation dependent PL emission characteristic of the synthesized CQDs has also been reported previously for different symmetric fluorescent carbon nanostructures [42,49].

However, to investigate the applicability of the synthesized luminescent material for sensing applications the fluorescent stability of the synthesized CQD sample has been studied in this work by changing the environmental conditions of the dispersing medium, such as (i) pH, (ii) ionic strength, and (iii) exposure to a certain radiant intensity of UV irradiation for a long period of time. Results of these investigations have been presented and discussed in details in Fig. S2a–c (see Supporting information). It has been found that PL emission intensity of the sample is the highest at physiological pH value of pH7 (Fig. S2a), suggesting that the luminescent material synthesized from bael leaves has potential application as a biosensing probe. Whereas, the rapid change in PL emission intensity of the synthesized carbon nanostructures with pH values suggests their application as a monitor of pH-sensitive chemical reactions. On the other hand, it has been found that the intensity and spectral nature of PL emission remains almost constant even in the presence of ionic liquid of variable strengths. It is found that as the concentration of KCl is increased upto 2.5M, only 5% change in PL emission intensity has been occurred (as shown in the inset of Fig. S2b). This result indicates towards the applicability of CQDs for the real-life fluorescence-based in-vivo sensing of metal ions in human serum and leaving cells [51].

The origin of tunable PL emission in fluorescent CQDs is a well-known debated topic till-to-date. There are reports with widely varying mechanisms, including that it arises from the intrinsic energy band (л-л⁎ ) of sp 2 carbon core, leading to quantum confinement effect and also due to extrinsic defects on the surface of synthesized carbon nanostructures [52]. However, in our present study, the red shift in position of λem of the synthesized CQDs obtained under excitation of wavelengths longer than 325nm has been assigned to the influence of polar solvent environment on the photo-excited electrons. Whereas, the observed change in PL emission intensity with variation of excitation wavelength in between 275 and 325nm is srongly related rather than sp2 hybridized graphitic core of the synthesized luminescent material, when dispersed in DI warer at a concentration higher than 0.06mgmL−1, due to the effect of rigidity aggregated crosslinking enhanced emission (RACEE), as discussed latter.

The excitation dependent PL emission spectra of the CQDs in a different polar dispersion medium, such as DI water, DMF, MeOH and EtOH, have been presented in Fig. S3a–d. As shown in Fig. S3a–d (in Supporting information), the amount of red shift in the position of λem , taken at excitation wavelengths larger than 325nm, is strongly dependent on the polarity of the solvent environment. A maximum red shift in PL emission center of ~0.40eV has been observed when the sample is dispersed in DI water (see Fig. S3a). When CQDs are dispersed in a highly polar solvent such as DI water, a relatively slow nonradiative relaxation process (due to long solvation) occurs between the excited energy levels at the same time scale as that of PL emission (10−9 s) and induces a red shift in position of peak PL emission, it is commonly known as “giant red edge effect” [23]. Consequently, the amount of red shift has been observed to be decreased with polarity of the solvent as depicted by the excitation-dependent PL emission spectra shown in Fig. S3a–d.

The effect of solvent polarity on delaying the nonradiative relaxation process has also been experimentally verified by the observed changes in the line shape of the PL emission spectra (Fig. 3c) of synthesized CQDs dispersed in different nonpolar (CH) and polar (EtOH and DI water) solvents, collected at fixed excitation wavelength of 325nm. The FWHM corresponding to each PL emission spectrum has been presented in the inset of Fig. 3c. Clearly, a relatively narrow band emission due to the homogenous broadening in PL emission spectra has been observed for CQDs when dispersed in nonpolar solvent CH in compared to those obtained in polar solvents (EtOH and DI water). Whereas, the broadband emission from CQDs dispersed in polar solvents is occurred due to the effect of inhomogenous broadening [24] in PL emission spectra, collected at an excitation wavelength of 325nm. The homogenous broadening of PL emission spectrum of the synthesized fluorophore in a nonpolar solvent is because of rapid nonradiative relaxation (~10−12 s) of excited electrons to band edge states before the PL emission as presented schematically in Fig. 3d. Usually PL emission process after such rapid nonradiative transitions will be in accordance with Kasha's rule [24,53] and the emission wavelength will be independent of excitation wavelength. But in the presence of an interacting environment like polar solvent a delayed nonradiative relaxation of excited electrons occurs during the solvation process and it induces an excitation dpendent red shift in the PL emission spectra of the fluorophore. After excitation, the fluorophore and the solvent dipole no longer remains in equilibrium state and change in orientation of the solvent dipole occurs which further leads to a reduction in energy of the fluorophore (Fig. 3d). As a result of such polarity sensitive solute-solvent interaction, the CQDs exhibits excitation dependent shifted PL emission property in an aqueous medium. Observation of such excitation wavelength dependent PL emission due to the effect of polary of the dispersant medium for luminescent carbon nanostructures namely, carbon nanodots (CNDs) and nano graphene oxide (GO) sheets have been reported earlier [23–25].

Moreover, the PLQY of the CQD sample in different polar dispersant media has been calculated in the present study and the results are tabulated in Table S1 (in Supporting information). The variation in PLQY of the sample with permittivity of the dispersant media has been presented in Fig. 3e and it can be fitted with an exponential decaying funtion, as given by,
(1a)
φ
ε
= 39.2 exp
− ε
10
+ 3.12 .

The value PLQY has been found to be increased from 3% to 22% when the dipersing medium is changed from DI water (ε =80) water to THF (ε =7.5), such a variation in PLQY with permittivity of the surrounding medium is strongly related to the solvation effect on nonradiative relaxation dynamics of the solvent-solute system in more polar solvents. The ratio of rate of nonradiative transition to radiative transition (knr/kr ) can be described by Eq. (1b) which is given as,
(1b)
φ =
1
1 +
k nr
k r
where, kr and knr are the radiative non-radiative rate constant, respectively and ϕ is the PLQY. Previously, Stsiapura et al. have reported that knr can be enhanced by 3–4 times with the increase in ε [26] . Therefore, PLQY can be decreased with the increase in ε, as has been observed experimentally in our CQDs sample. Moreover, we have also observed a perceptible change in the visible blue luminescence intesity of the sample with the change in the dispersing medium under 365nm UV irradiation, as shown in the inset of Fig. 3e. This observation indicates towards potential application of CQDs for visual permittivity detection [54]. The solvent sensitive PL emission behavior of the synthesized luminescent carbon nanostructures has motivated us to study visible light emission characteristics of CQDs in the presence of polymer matrix for their future photonic applications in solid form and to investigate their solvatochromic PL emission behavior in details for sensing applications.

On the other hand, to justify the effect of surface functional groups on PL emission of synthesized CQDs, the concentration dependent steady-state PL emission characteristics of the sample has been studied. The room temperature PL emission spectra have been collected at an excitation wavelength of 325nm after dispersing the synthesized CQD sample in an aqueous medium in different concentration and shown in Fig. S4a (in Supporting information). Surprisingly, an additional PL emission peak centered at ~370nm along-with a broad emission band centered at ~430nm have been appeared at lower concentration (0.02–0.06mgmL−1) of the sample (Fig. S4b–d). The integrated PL emission intensity has been found to be increased with increase in concentration of CQDs (see Fig. S5a, in Supporting information). In order to find the origin of PL emission peak in the UV region, the PL emission spectrum of the synthesized sample has been recorded at a lower excitation wavelength of 275nm and 300nm (Fig S5b–c). As shown in the PL emission spectra in Fig. S5b–c, the deconvoluted peak centered at ~370nm (peak1) has been aroused due to the graphitic sp2 carbon core by direct electron-hole recombination process. Whereas, the broad emission band centered at ~430nm (peak 2) (in Fig. S5b–c) is due to the contribution from the functional groups present on the surface of the synthesized carbon nanostructures [55]. It is worthy to note that, the PL emission has been dominated by the surface states over the graphitic carbon core at an excitation wavelength higher than 275nm and the emission intensity has become the highest at an excitation wavelength of 325nm.

At lower concentration, the possibility of the non-radiative decay of free electrons, presents within the functional groups (commonly known as sub-fluorophores [56]), by means of rotational and vibrational relaxations. As a consequence, contribution from graphitic carbon core becomes a dominating factor for the observed PL emission at a lower concentration of the sample. Whereas, as the number of the sub-fluorophore is increased at higher concentration, the possibility of such non-radiative decay of the free electrons is decreased due to the crosslinking between these sub-fluorophores which are commonly known as rigidity aggregated crosslinking enhanced emission (RACEE) effect [56]. Such observation in PL emission due to RACEE is quite frequent in conjugated as well as non-conjugated fluorescent polymer structures and the enhancement in PL emission induced by RACEE effect in case of polymer carbon nanodots has been reported earlier by Lai et al. [57] .

Moreover, in the present study, the effects of organic liquids on the visible luminescence of CQDs in presence of solid polymer matrix have also been investigated. The emission spectrum of solid flexible PVA-CQD films under UV irradiation has been collected by using optical fiber spectrometer (AvaSpec-2048) after immersing them in different organic liquid (DI water, DMSO, EtOH, and THF) for 1min. A schematic of the indigenously developed experimental setup for the measurement of emission spectra has been presented in Fig. 4 . Similar to the CQDs dispersed in liquid (shown in inset of Fig. 3e), the solvent dependent variation in intensity of blue luminescence of the PVA-CQD films have also been observed when immersed in different organic liquids and thereafter exposed to the UV radiation. Clearly, the PVA-CQD film immersed in THF exhibits maximum intensity in compared to those when immersed in EtOH, DMSO and DI Water as depicted in the inset (a) of Fig. 4. The digital photographs presented in the inset (b) of Fig. 4 (in top middle) shows the luminescent PVA-CQD films under 365nm UV irradiation placed in a lab environment (air) (i) and after immersing them in different organic liquids for 1min. (ii-v). The effect of liquid environment on the blue luminescence intensity of the PVA-CQD film has also been reflected in the digital photographs of the luminescent PVA-CQD films. Development of such fluorescent molecules embedded in a nano-fibrous polymer for sensing ethanol in alcoholic beverages has been recently reported by Aakamatsu et al. [58] . On the other hand, on-off-on PL sensing of fluoride ions by using fluorescent CDs in agarose gel bead has been demonstrated by Basu et al. [59].

Interestingly the shape of the solid luminescent polymer films i.e., PVA-CQD films remains unaltered after being immersed in EtOH, DMSO, and THF. Whereas, the PVA-CQD films has been swelled when immersed in water (ii) for 1min as shown in the inset (b) of Fig. 4. This modification in the shape of PVA-CQD film has been attributed to the strong interaction of hydrophilic PVA monomers with water [60] which acts as a plasticizer for PVA matrix. As a consequence, the hydrogen bonds between PVA monomers and CO groups of CQDs get deformed in presence of water. Therefore, the luminescence of the PVA-CQD film has been quenched drastically after being immersed in water. On the other hand, organic liquids such as EtOH, DMSO, and THF diffuse through PVA matrix without affecting the shape of PVA-CQD film. Thus the independent interaction between solvent molecules (EtOH, DMSO, and THF) with surface moieties of CQDs encapsulated within the PVA matrix offers the solvent dependent variation in the intensity of emitted light from solid PVA-CQD films under 365nm UV irradiation as shown in Fig. 4 (inset (b)). Such diffusion of organic liquids through PVA matrix and their interaction with reduced graphene oxide (r-GO) in case of the PVA-rGO composite system has also been reported by Liu et al. [61]. Therefore, the present study on solvent dependent PL emission behavior of the solid PVA-CQD film under UV excitation suggest their application as solid state photonic sensor for the detection of polar organic liquids.

The effects of solvent on PL emission behavior of the synthesized sample have been investigated by dispersing the sample in seven different solvents, and the measurement of PL emission spectra have been carried out with a sample concentration of 0.1mgmL−1 and at an excitation photon energy of 3.82eV (325nm). The solvation capabilities of the solvents used in the present experiment have been listed in the Table 1 . The normalized PL emission spectra of CQDs dispersed in nonpolar (CH), polar aprotic (DMF, THF, and Ace) and polar protic (DI water, EtOH, and MeOH) solvents have been presented in Fig. 5a The PL emission spectra exhibit a strong solvent dependent shift in PL peak emission center as can be seen in Fig. 5a. It has been found that the PL emission center has been over-all red shifted by an amount of 0.32eV (~2575cm−1) with an increase in solvent polarity and it is associated with the stabilization of emissive energy states of the synthesized sample during solvation [40]. Such solvent-sensitive linear optical characteristic i.e., the solvatochromic PL emission behavior of synthesized CQDs is similar to that commonly observed in fluorescent organic dyes, which have been reported earlier [31]. Moreover, unlike the case of previously reported result [37], no substantial change in the line-shape of the PL emission spectra of the CQDs has been observed in the present study except in case with CH.

In the present study, the SPL emission properties of the CQDs have been studied by using the so-called ET N scale [62], a normalized polarity parameter measuring the overall solvation capability of the solvents in terms of specific (hydrogen bond; HB) and nonspecific (e.g., dipole-dipole) solute-solvent interactions. The emission peak position of the CQDs dispersed in different solvents has been found to exhibit a linear dependency (R 2 =0.9532) on ET
N , as shown in Fig. 5b and has been fitted by using the following equation,
(2)
E p
= − 0.230
E N T
+ 3.04 .

Here, Ep is the energy corresponding to the peak position of the PL emission spectra (in Fig. 5a) of CQDs dispersed in different solvents. This result highlights the possible application of synthesized luminescent carbon nanostructures for fluorescence sensing of organic solvents in pure as well as in the form of a binary aqueous mixture. Furthermore, in order to investigate the possible interactions responsible for the observed solvatochromic red-shifted PL emission, we have judiciously compared the PL spectra of the CQDs dispersed in some selected liquid media as depicted in Fig. 5c. The observed SPL emission behavior of the CQD sample has been attributed [63] to the combined effects due to change in dipole interaction described by Lippert-Mataga model as well as HB interaction between the solvent molecule and surface moieties present within the synthesized sample. According to the surface chemistry analysis presented by the XPS and FTIR data as shown in Fig. 2d–f, the luminescent CQDs synthesized from Bael leaves consist of abundant oxygen and nitrogen containing carboxylic and amine functional groups on the surface of the sp2 hybridized graphitic core. These functional groups act as a major contributor for achieving observed luminescence as discussed earlier in this report. Fig. 5c clearly exhibits that the HB interaction between the solvent molecule and the solute i.e., CQDs is mostly dominated by the HB donating capability (customarily quantified by the Kamlet-Taft parameter [64], α) of polar protic solvents rather than the aprotic one. The emission spectrum of CQDs dispersed in a nonpolar solvent, such as CH (ε =0.046,α =0) has been considered as a reference point as shown in Fig. 5c. Although there is a large change in dielectric property of the solvent environment when the sample has been dispersed in polar aprotic solvents such as DMF (ε =38,α =0), a red shift in PL emission peak position (Fig. 5c) of amount 0.27eV with respect to the reference solvent (CH) has been observed for the polar solvent DMF.

However, the amount of red shift in PL emission has been comparatively increased when the sample has been dispersed in polar protic solvents, such as MeOH (ε =33,α =0.98) and DI-water (ε =80,α =1.17) and the shift becomes=0.37eV, the highest when the sample is dispersed in DI water. Hence, the origin of SPL emission of the synthesized sample dispersed in polar protic solvents can be attributed to the HB interaction between the protic solvent to the CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Origin of SPL Emission in CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to explore the possible reason behind the solvatochromic behavior of the synthesized material we have utilized also the UV–vis absorption spectroscopic data of the sample. Absorption is a very fast process (occurs within a time scale of fs.) compared to PL, so it can be advantageously used towards determining the electronic energy level of a material by ignoring the solvent reorientation effect [65]. The UV–vis absorption data of the CQD sample, dispersed in different nonpolar, polar aprotic and polar protic solvents, have been measured at room temperature. The deconvoluted UV–vis absorption spectra of the CQDs in different solvents are presented in Fig. S6 (in Supporting information). Fig. S6 shows the presences of two absorption peaks (peak 1 and peak 2) in the wavelength region of 250–350nm. As discussed earlier, peak 1 corresponds to π-π* transition and peak 2 corresponds to the n-π* transition occurred within the sample. On the other hand, in the lower energy region, i.e. in the visible wavelength region (with λ ˃ 350nm) a broad absorption tail is present. This broad absorption tail which is extended up to 600nm is attributed to the formation of multiple energy bands within the sample dispersed in different solvents [66]. The spectral positions of the UV absorption peaks have been summarized in Table 1. As is found from Table 1 that spectral positions of peak 1 (denoted by λa ) and polarity redistribution function, f(n) are inversely related for polar aprotic solvents (such as, THF and DMF), which is in accordance to the Lippert- Mataga model [63,65] described by Eq. (6), whereby the CQDs-solvent system is assumed as a diatomic molecule. This result indicates that no HB interaction has taken place between CQDs and polar aprotic solvents and thus the formation of uniform Onsager reaction field has taken place due to the effect of dipole-dipole interaction around the fluorophore. The difference in energy (ΔE) of absorption peak 1 and PL emission peak of the sample can be related to the ground state (δg ) and excited state (δe ) dipole moment of the solute and other properties of the bulk solvent as given below [65].

where, f(ε,n) is known as Lippert-Mataga polarity function is a function of refractive index (n) and dielectric constant (ε) of the solvent and it defines orientation polarizability of the solvent [67]. Considering the high-frequency component only, the Lippert-Mataga polarity function will reduce to [65],

where, a is known as Onsager cavity radius. The value of a is equal to r0 , the radius of a hard sphere (HS) dipolar solute bearing point dipoles and is independent of solvent property, such as dielectric constant and polarity of the solvent [68]. In the present study, we have considered the Onsager cavity radius equals to 3nm, the radius of a single CQD as obtained from the TEM image, shown in Fig. 2b.

On the other hand, the interaction between the protic solvents and solute is comparably complex in nature. As a consequence, it is difficult to explain the observed solvatochromic effect only by using Lippert-Mataga model due to the non-uniform distribution of reaction field unlike the case of aprotic solvents. From the Table 1 we have observed that the variation of λa is directly proportional to HB donating capability (α) and inversely proportional to f(n) of the polar protic solvents. This observation suggests the presence of specific interaction namely HB interaction followed by dipole-dipole interaction of CQDs in polar protic solvents. Therefore, a non-uniform distribution of reaction field will be occurred around the solute (CQD) as proposed by Mukherjee et al. [63]. This has been further confirmed by analysing the change in dipole moment (Δδ) due to solute-solvent interaction under optical excitation, estimated by using different bulk solvent polarity functions and in terms of molecular-microscopic solvent polarity parameter (ET N ) (as discussed latter in this context). Being a charge mediated process, strong HB interaction acts as a major contributor to control the band gap of the CQDs during their interaction with polar protic solvents along with feeble dipole interactions. To give a notion of specific (HB) and non-specific (dipole-dipole) interactions on the observed solvatochromic behavior of the synthesized carbon nanostructured material, the values of δg , δe and their changes (Δδ) for the synthesized CQDs have been calculated in the present study by using different solvatochromic model as discussed in the Supporting information in details. The values of δg and δe of the synthesized CQDs have been calculated by using Bakhshiev and Kawski–Chamma–Viallet solvatochromic models for bulk solvents (See Fig. S7 in Supporting information) and are found out to be 14 Debye and 81 Debye, respectively. The calculated value of δg and δe for CQDs are comparable with the semiconductor quantum dots of ZnO as reported recently by Pal et al. [69].

On the other hand, the value of Δδ has been estimated by using Lippert-Mataga, Bakhshiev polarity functions and molecular-macroscopic polarity relation (ET N vs. stokes shift) developed by Rabi et al. [70], and presented in Table S3 (in Supporting information). The moderately high values of Δδ estimated from the polarity functions suggest that, the fluorescence emission from the CQDs is related to an excited state, which is more polarized than the ground-state. However, It is observed that the value of Δδ (=133 Debye) estimated by using Lippert-Mataga polarity function is large compared to those estimated from by other polarity functions (Table S3). This is due to the fact that, the Lippert-Mataga polarity function is entirely related to solute-solvent dipole interactions [71]. Whereas, the value of Δδ (=38 Debye) estimated by using ET N vs. stoke shift is much smaller than the values estimated from bulk solvent polarity functions (Table S3). This result suggests that the presence of intense effect of short range specific interactions between the protic polar solvents and CQDs (due to HB) compared to non-specific interaction [67].

The profound effect of HB interaction over the dipole interaction to describe the observed solvatochromism of CQDs in presence of polar protic solvents has been further verified by the PL emission spectra as presented in Fig. S8a (in Supporting information). The synthesized CQD sample not only exhibits enhancement in PL emission intensity in EtOH and MeOH, but also a relative shift of 0.01eV in PL emission peak position has been observed which is 11 times less than those for CQDs dispersed in DMF and DMSO (0.11eV) (see Fig. S8a). This immovability of PL emission spectra and enhancement in intensity of PL emission of the synthesized sample in alcoholic (EtOH, MeOH) media have been occurred due to the stability in excited states through the preferential HB interaction of OH groups of alcoholic solvents with the carbonyl groups (C=O) of CQDs unlike in other non-alcholic environment such as DMF and DMSO [66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of EtOH and THF in Aqueous Binary Mixture
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to find the possible application of this solvatochromic behavior of the synthesized sample for the detection of organic liquids, we have also investigated their PL emission behavior in presence two different aqueous binary mixture i.e., DI water-EtOH and DI water-THF. Fig. 5d depicts the variation in PL emission peak position of CQDs dispersed in aqueous binary mixture of EtOH (dotted line in black) along-with the variations of theoretically calculated (solid line in blue) and experimentally (dashed line in blue) obtained values of molar electronic transition energy (ET(m) ) of the binary mixture with molar fraction (χOS ) of the bulk solvent (EtOH). Similar variation in PL emission peak position and ET(m) with χOS has been observed in Fig. S8b (Supporting information) when CQDs are dispersed in aqueous binary mixture of THF. The theoretical values of ET(m) have been calculated by using Eq. (7), and the experimental values of ET(m) have been estimated by using Eqs. (2) and (8) [72],

Here, χW is the molar fraction of water, ET(OS) is the molar electronic transition energy (kcalmol−1) for organic solvents and ET(W) is the same for water (corresponding values has been given in Table 1. The values of molecular-microscopic solvent polarity parameter for the binary mixtures (ET N(mixture)) has been calculated by using Eq. (2) and with the energy value corresponding to the PL emission peak positions of CQDs dispersed in the aqueous binary mixtures containing different molar fractions (χOS) of organic solvents (EtOH and THF).

Remarkably, in case of both aqueous binary mixtures an irregularity in the variation of experimental values of ET(m) with χOS has been observed (plotted with dashed line in blue in Fig. 5d and Fig. S8b). Unlike the theoretical values (plotted with solid line in blue) of the ET(m) , the experimental values of ET(m) (plotted with dashed line in blue) does not vary linearly with increase in χOS , but only up to a certain lower value of χOS . Such anomalous variation in experimental values of ET(m) for a mixed solvent system estimated from solvatochromic spectral shift of traditional dyes is a common feature and has been reported by many other research groups including Dawber et al. [72], and it is occurred due to the preferential solvation effect of the solute molecule to the more polar component of the binary mixture. In order to estimate the degree of preferential solvation of synthesized CQDs in a given composition of aqueous binary mixtures of EtOH and water quantitatively we have calculated the preferential “solvation factor”(η) from the ET(m) vs. χ w (molar fraction of water) plot by interpolating on the linear ideal mixture line as presented in Fig. S8c–d (in Supporting information). Where, η =1 corresponds to random solvation or non-specific solvation by either component of the binary solvent mixture, η >1 corresponds to the preferential solvation by the more polar component of the binary solvent mixture and η <1 corresponds to the preferential solvation by the less polar component of the binary solvent mixture [72]. The preferential “solvation factor” of polar liquid has been represented by the Eq. (9) [72]. Where, ET1(m) molar electronic transition energy estimated experimentally due to solvatochromic PL emissive behavior of the solute (CQDs) for a given molar fraction of water (χw1 ) and ET2(m) is the molar transition energy of the aqueous binary mixture solvent system estimated from linear ideal mixture line at another molar fraction (χw2 ). The solvation factor η can be given as,

Here, the values of η have been calculated by using Eq. (9) corresponding to different values of χw and it is found to be <1 for aqueous binary mixtures of EtOH as well as that of THF (see Table S4, in Supporting information). This result confirms that the synthesized CQDs have been preferentially solvated by the less polar component (EtOH and THF) of the binary mixtures.

Recently, there has been a plethora of investigations on how CQDs can be used as sensing probes for detection of neighbouring ions and molecules. Sciortino et al. [40], has proposed the use of solvatochromic CDs as sensing probe for ion and molecular detection, has propelled us to investigate the of ability our own CQDs as sensing probe for metal ion detection.
In recent years fluorescence-based metal ion sensing using highly luminescent nanomaterials has drawn a momentous research interest for their uses in daily life application.

Here, we have measured PL emission properties of CQDs in presence of different metal ions, at an excitation wavelength (λex ) of 325nm, and demonstrated sensing of Fe3+ by using a convenient “mix and detect” [3] protocol. Fig. 6a depicts the relative change in PL emission intensity of aqueous solution of the synthesized CQDs under optimized conditions (0.1mgmL−1, pH7) loaded with different metal ions including Zn2+, Ag+, Al3+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+ (each having concentration of 1mM). A maximum quenching of ~30% in PL emission intensity of the CQDs loaded with Fe 3+ has been observed to that of the unloaded CQDs in an aqueous medium (Fig. 6a). This result suggests the selectivity of the synthesized luminescent CQDs for the detection of Fe3+ ions.

The PL emission spectra of CQDs loaded with different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–300μM) are shown in Fig. 6b which are measured at room temperature at an excitation wavelength of 325nm. And Fig. 6b exhibits a continual quenching of PL emission intensity of CQDs with an increase in concentration of Fe3+ ions. If fluorescence quenching of CQDs occurs due to either static or dynamic quenching mechanism, it will follow the Stern–Volmer equation, [73]
(10)

where, the Stern–Volmer quenching constant KSV =KS for static quenching and KSV =K D for dynamic quenching, [Fe3+ ] is the quencher (Fe3+) concentration, and I0 and I are the PL intensities of CQDs at 432nm in the absence and presence of quencher (Fe3+), respectively.

By observing the Fig. 6c, minutely it can be found that there are different trends of variations of (I0/I) for different ranges of Fe3+ concentration, although apparently it looks like a linear variation in the whole range. We have also studied the variation of the UV–vis absorption characteristics of CQDs loaded with various concentrations of Fe3+ and results are shown in Fig. 6d as well as in Fig. S9. In Fig. S9 the absorbance curves are plotted for a smaller wavelength range of 250–300nm for better observations. Fig. 6d and the inset of Fig. S9 shows that at first with the increase of Fe3+ ion concentration upto 20μM the absorbance is decreased and then it is increased. Similar observations on absorption characteristics of CQD/Fe3+ complex system have been reported earlier by Song et al. [74]. In Fig. 6e(i) and (ii) we have shown the fitting of the experimental data of I0/I for different ranges of Fe3+ concentration. Fig. 6e(i) shows that the experimental points are best fitted (R2
=0.9851) with the Eq. (11) in the lower concentration range of 0–20μM of Fe3+ and obtained a sensitivity of 0.0062μM−1 for detection of Fe3+.

On the basis of Eq. (11), the detection limit (LOD) is estimated [42] to be 0.12μM at a signal to noise ratio of 3 (number of observation, n =7). In the lower range of concentration (0–20μM), the complex formation between CQDs and Fe3+ ions takes place as discussed latter. Whereas, at the higher concentration range (20˂[Fe3+ ]≤250μM) of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity has been proposed to be quenched due to the combined effect of static quenching (complex formation) and dynamic quenching (collisions). In this case, the general Stern–Volmer equation can be written as [65],

Notably, the linear range of detection of Fe3+ ions and LOD is comparable to and even better than the previously reported results (see Table. 2 ). Also from literature studies it has been found that CQD based fluorescent sensing probes synthesized from bio-precursors are showing better performances, in some cases, in compared those which are obtained chemically (see Table 2).

The presence of different functional groups on the surface of graphitic carbon core of CQDs, synthesized by using bio-based precursors [82–84] as well as in the present work, have been assigned as the possible reason for achieving such high value of LOD for sensing of Fe3+ in CQDs. The presences of different functional groups in our synthesized samples have been confirmed by XPS and FTIR studies (as shown earlier in Fig. 1d–f). In the present work, we have used Aegle marmelos leave powder as a precursor for synthesis of CQDs which contains compounds, like alkaloids, carboxylic acids, phenols, sterols and xanthoproteins [86] in large amount. These compounds may act as in-situ source of multifunctional groups (COOH, OH, CHO, NH2 and CO-) during the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs. Significantly, the value of LOD of the synthesized luminescent metal ion sensing system is much lower than the maximum permissible limit (5.36μM) of Fe3+ ions in drinking water as per the guideline prescribed by WHO [42]. Therefore, to investigate the practicality, the developed method for detection of Fe3+ ions using fluorescent CQDs has been extended to determine the concentration of Fe3+ ions in environmental samples. Tap water sample collected from our Nanoscience Laboratory (Sample 1) and Department of Physics (Sample 2) have been filtered using 0.22μM membrane filters followed by centrifugation at 12000rpm for 10min to remove the large suspended particles and used for quantitative analysis of Fe3+ ions present in it. Initially, the concentration of Fe3+ ions presents within the water sample has been determined using the proposed method without adding a standard solution of Fe3+ ions. Then the different concentration of Fe3+ ions (50μM, 100μM, and 200μM) has been added to the environmental water sample and PL emission spectra have been measured at room temperature.

The PL emission spectra (λex
=325nm) of the synthesized CQDs (0.1mgmL−1) dissolved in tap water samples have been recorded (see Fig. S10a–b in Supporting information) after spiking with standard solutions of Fe3+ at different concentrations (0–200μM). The results presented in Table 3 depicts that the measured amount of Fe3+ are in great agreement with the added content. The recoveries of Fe3+ in all real samples have been calculated [12] and presented in the Table 3. The recoveries of all sample are found to be in the range of 100–102% and the low relative slandered deviations (RSDs), ranging from 0.99% to 1.96% suggests that the present method for the detection of Fe3+ has good accuracy. In addition, the simple, low-cost eco-friendly synthesis of the sensing probe i.e., fluorescent CQDs is favourable for its further application and development.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of Fe3+ Sensing Using CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To study the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CQDs in presence of Fe3+ ions, the fluorescence emission behavior of Fe3+/CQD system has been further studied in presence of l-cysteine. The UV–vis absorption spectra in presence of different concentration of Fe3+ (0–250μM) and depicted in Fig. 6d and in Fig. S9. At first with the increase in concentration of Fe3+ upto 20μM the absorbance is found to be decreased, as discussed earlier. Whereas, an obvious enhancement in absorbance has been observed when CQDs are loaded with Fe3+ having concentrations >20μM (inset of Fig. S9, Supporting information). This result suggests the formation of Fe3+/CQD complex system when concentration of Fe3+ ions is low (≤20μΜ) and the photon absorption is dominated by Fe3+ ions in higher concentration [74]. The existence of photoactive aromatic hydroxyl groups on the surface of synthesized sample (as discussed earlier) and the UV–vis absorption characteristics are shown in Fig. 6d which has confirmed the coordination of Fe3+ ions with luminescent CQDs. The as formed Fe3+/CQD complexes are responsible for the transfer of electrons from HOMO (singlet excited state) of excited CQDs to the half field 3d orbital of Fe3+ ions, is commonly known as photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [51]. Therefore, in the present study, the acceptor Fe3+ ions act as a PL emission quencher. Whereas, the PL emission intensity of the fluorescent CQDs loaded with Fe3+ quencher has been partially recovered with the addition of l-cysteine (100μM) which has been depicted by the PL emission spectra in Fig. 6f. l-Cysteine has been used for the restoration of PL emission of CQDs so that it can provide OH– ions to take away Fe3+ ions from the CQD/Fe3+ complex [74], as shown schematically in Fig. 7

In summary, here we have reported a simple and facile hydrothermal technique for synthesis of a carbon based fluorescent nanostructure, namely CQDs from Aegle marmelos leave, a naturally available green precursor material. The CQDs thus synthesized exhibit excitation dependent PL emission property with the highest PLQY of 22% and a considerable enhancement in PLQY by a factor of 7 is demonstrated when the synthesized sample has been dispersed in THF as compared to that in DI water. The PLQY follows an exponentially decaying nature with the increase in relative permittivity of the dispersing medium, suggesting their application as visual permittivity detection probe in future. The photo-physical stability of CQDs monitored at physiological pH value (pH7) and in presence of wide range of ionic (KCl) concentrations unravels their suitability for using in-vivo as well as in-vitro bio-sensing. The rarely reported SPL emissive behavior of CQDs has been demonstrated here comprehensively in several bulk organic dispersing media as well as in aqueous binary mixture of solvents. The value of change in dipole moments (Δδ) during the solute-solvent interactions has been calculated and it is found that when only the effect of non-specific dipole-dipole interaction has been considered the value of Δδ is about 2–4 times higher in compared that when specific HB interaction is considered. The observed solvatochromic response of the synthesized luminophore has been assigned to the combined effect of dipole-dipole interaction along-with the HB interaction between the solute-solvent systems. Although, the use of fluorescent carbon nanostructures having SPL emission property as a sensing probe for ion and molecular detection have been proposed earlier, herein we have demonstrated it experimentally. The synthesized sample has been used as a probe for sensing organic liquids, namely EtOH and THF in the aqueous binary mixture, with a LOD of 0.9% and 1% molar fraction for THF and EtOH, respectively. The extensive solvatochromic response of the synthesized sample indicates its future applicability in bio-sensing. The observed change in light emission intensity of the synthesized PVA-CQDs film with the polarity of the organic liquid environment indicates that the synthesized CQDs could be used as a luminescent solid state polarity sensor in future. Finally, Fe3+ ion sensing even in laboratory tap water has been carried out and achieved the highest LOD of 0.12 μΜ. Thus the present work on the synthesis of highly emissive CQDs from Aegle marmelos leaves has opened up an outright eco-friendly avenue to convert a bio-waste into an attractive luminescent material (i.e. to a bio-asset) to be used in luminescence based multiple sensing applications.

Authors are thankful to CSIR, Govt. of India for the financial grant no. 03(1328)/14/EMR-II, dt. 03.11.2014. AP is also thankful to CSIR for the maintenance scholarship. Authors are thankful to Central Research Facility (CRF), IIT Kharagpur for TEM, Raman spectroscopic characterizations and the DST-FIST funded XPS facility at Department of Physics and Meteorology, IIT Kharagpur for XPS characterization. Authors are thankful to the reviewers for their fruitful suggestions. AP is also thankful to Dr. A. K. Kole and Mr. Partha Kumbhakar for reading the manuscript and for their help.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2017.10.054.
